questions.question_text: Under the standard Georgia Association of REALTORS (GAR) purchase and sale agreement, within how many banking days of the binding agreement date must earnest money be deposited into the trust account?
distractors: Three banking days
Seven calendar days
Ten business days
questions.explanation: The GAR purchase and sale agreement directs the broker to deposit earnest money within five banking days of the binding agreement date unless the contract specifies otherwise. GREC Rule 520-1-.08 itself uses an 'as soon after receipt as is practicably possible' standard with no fixed banking-day count, so the five-day figure most candidates memorize comes from the standard GAR contract rather than the rule.

questions.question_text: A standard GAR purchase and sale agreement becomes binding on Thursday. The parties did not modify the form's default earnest money deposit deadline. By when must the holder deposit the earnest money?
questions.correct_answer: By the end of the following Thursday
distractors: By the end of the following Monday
Within 72 hours of the binding agreement
By the end of the current business week
questions.explanation: The GAR contract's default requires deposit within five banking days of the Binding Agreement Date. Counting five banking days from Thursday (Friday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday), the deposit is due by the end of the following Thursday, excluding weekends and bank holidays. Absent such a contract provision, GREC Rule 520-1-.08 simply requires deposit as soon after receipt as is practicably possible.

questions.question_text: Where must a Georgia broker deposit earnest money received in a real estate transaction?
questions.correct_answer: A trust or escrow account at a Georgia bank
distractors: The broker's business operating account
Any FDIC-insured account in any state
A GREC-managed statewide escrow fund
questions.explanation: Georgia requires earnest money to be deposited in a trust or escrow account at a financial institution located in Georgia. Using the broker's operating account or an out-of-state account would violate trust account requirements.

questions.question_text: A salesperson in Georgia receives an earnest money check from a buyer. Who is authorized to disburse funds from the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: The broker or a broker-designated person only
distractors: Any licensed salesperson in the firm
The buyer who originally provided the funds
The closing attorney handling the transaction
questions.explanation: Only the broker or a person specifically designated by the broker may disburse funds from the trust account. Salespersons are not independently authorized to make disbursements, ensuring accountability and oversight of client funds.

questions.question_text: A purchase contract specifies that earnest money will be deposited within ten days of the binding agreement. The standard GAR form's default is five banking days. Which deadline controls?
distractors: The five-banking-day rule always overrides
The shorter of the two deadlines applies
GREC must approve any extension beyond five days
questions.explanation: The parties' contract controls the deposit deadline. The five-banking-day figure is only the GAR form's default, and the parties may agree to a longer or shorter period. GREC Rule 520-1-.08 itself requires deposit as soon after receipt as is practicably possible when the contract is silent; it imposes no fixed banking-day count and GREC approval is never required.

questions.question_text: A Georgia broker keeps $100 of personal funds in the trust account to cover bank service charges. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, a reasonable amount for bank fees is allowed
distractors: No, any personal funds in the trust account is commingling
Only with prior written approval from GREC
Yes, up to $500 is permitted in the trust account
questions.explanation: GREC Rule 520-1-.08 lets a broker keep personal funds in the trust account only when clearly identified as the broker's deposit and only to meet the bank's minimum balance requirement or a reasonable amount to cover service and other bank charges. The rule sets no dollar cap — $100 is merely a common practice figure — and any broker funds beyond what is needed must be removed.

questions.question_text: A GREC investigator arrives unannounced at a brokerage and requests to inspect trust account records. Can the broker refuse access?
questions.correct_answer: No, records must be available for GREC inspection anytime
distractors: Yes, GREC must schedule inspections in advance
Yes, the broker may request a 48-hour notice period
Only if the broker's attorney is not present
questions.explanation: Trust account records must be available for GREC inspection at any time. Brokers cannot refuse or delay access. This ensures regulatory oversight and protects consumer funds held in trust.

questions.question_text: How long must a Georgia broker retain trust account records including deposits, disbursements, and reconciliations?
distractors: A minimum of five years
A minimum of one year
Until the end of the current license cycle
questions.explanation: Georgia requires trust account records to be retained for a minimum of three years. These records must include all deposits, disbursements, and reconciliation statements, and must be available for GREC inspection at any time.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits a commission check into the trust account instead of the operating account. After realizing the error three days later, what is the broker's situation?
questions.correct_answer: The broker has commingled funds and must correct it
distractors: No violation occurred since the funds are identifiable
This is permitted for up to five banking days
Only a violation if the funds remain over 30 days
questions.explanation: Depositing commission or operating funds into the trust account is commingling, which is strictly prohibited even if accidental. The broker must immediately correct the error by transferring the commission to the proper operating account.

questions.question_text: A Georgia broker uses one bank account for both client trust funds and brokerage operating expenses. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Commingling of trust and operating funds
distractors: Failure to maintain FDIC insurance coverage
Operating without a proper business license
Violating the five-day deposit requirement
questions.explanation: Mixing client trust funds with operating funds in a single account is commingling, strictly prohibited under Georgia law. Client funds must be kept in a separate trust or escrow account, distinct from any operating or personal accounts.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller dispute who is entitled to the earnest money after a failed transaction. The broker cannot determine who should receive the funds. What is an appropriate option?
questions.correct_answer: File an interpleader action with the court
distractors: Return the funds to the buyer automatically
Split the funds equally between both parties
Deposit the funds into the broker's operating account
questions.explanation: When parties dispute earnest money and the broker cannot resolve it, filing an interpleader action asks the court to decide who receives the funds. The broker cannot unilaterally disburse disputed funds to either party.

questions.question_text: During an earnest money dispute, a seller demands the broker release all funds immediately. The buyer has not agreed. What must the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Hold the funds until both parties agree or a court orders
distractors: Release the funds to the seller as the listing client
Return the funds to the buyer who made the deposit
Deduct the commission and split the remainder
questions.explanation: A broker may not simply hand disputed earnest money to the demanding party. Under GREC Rule 520-1-.08, the broker holds the funds until all parties sign a written agreement or a court orders disbursement, files an interpleader action, or disburses based upon a reasonable interpretation of the contract — in which case the broker must promptly notify all parties in writing (the GAR forms give the parties a 10-day objection period first). GREC itself never holds disputed funds.

questions.question_text: A Georgia broker holds disputed earnest money. Besides filing an interpleader action or holding the funds until the parties agree or a court orders, what third option does Georgia law give the broker?
questions.correct_answer: Disburse the funds based on a reasonable interpretation of the contract
distractors: Deposit the funds into a personal savings account
Transfer the funds to the listing agent's trust account
Donate the funds to a state housing charity
questions.explanation: GREC Rule 520-1-.08(3) permits a broker to disburse disputed trust funds upon a reasonable interpretation of the contract that directed the deposit. The broker must promptly notify all parties in writing of the disbursal, and the GAR forms require prior written notice with a 10-day objection period. GREC does not hold or escrow disputed funds — the other choices would all be conversion or commingling violations.

questions.question_text: A transaction falls through and both the buyer and seller claim the $10,000 earnest money. With no supporting basis in the contract and without giving the parties any written notice, the broker gives it all to the buyer. What is the consequence?
questions.correct_answer: The broker made an improper disbursement of disputed trust funds
distractors: No violation since the buyer originally deposited the funds
A violation only if the broker failed to notify GREC
No violation if done within five banking days of the dispute
questions.explanation: A broker may disburse disputed earnest money only on the grounds in GREC Rule 520-1-.08(3): a signed agreement of all parties, court order, interpleader, or a reasonable interpretation of the contract followed by prompt written notice to all parties. Paying one claimant with no contractual basis and no notice is an improper disbursement that exposes the broker to disciplinary action. GREC does not hold disputed funds, and the depositor has no automatic right to their return.

questions.question_text: After filing an interpleader action for disputed earnest money, who ultimately decides the distribution of the funds?
questions.correct_answer: The court determines the rightful recipient
distractors: GREC appoints an arbitrator to decide
The qualifying broker makes the final determination
The parties must agree or funds are forfeited
questions.explanation: In an interpleader action, the broker deposits the disputed funds with the court, which then determines who is entitled to receive them. This removes the broker from the decision and lets a neutral judicial authority resolve the dispute.
